nodes.label: C1.2 Cell Respiration
nodes.description: The controlled release of energy from organic compounds to produce ATP. This overarching topic is divided into several major interconnected concepts that occur across different cellular compartments.

nodes.label: Glycolysis
nodes.description: An anaerobic process occurring in the cytoplasm. It breaks down a 6-carbon glucose molecule into two 3-carbon pyruvate molecules. First, in phosphorylation, hexokinase uses two ATP molecules to add phosphate groups to glucose, making it unstable. During lysis, this hexose bisphosphate splits into two triose phosphate molecules. In the oxidation phase, triose phosphate loses hydrogen, reducing NAD+ to NADH. Finally, enzymes transfer phosphate groups directly to ADP, generating a net yield of two ATP via substrate-level phosphorylation.

nodes.label: The Link Reaction
nodes.description: If oxygen is present, pyruvate enters the mitochondrial matrix via transport proteins. Here, it undergoes oxidative decarboxylation. Pyruvate loses a carbon atom as carbon dioxide and is simultaneously oxidized, reducing NAD+ to NADH. The remaining 2-carbon acetyl group binds to Coenzyme A, forming Acetyl CoA.
nodes.pathwaySteps: Pyruvate enters mitochondrial matrix -> Oxidative Decarboxylation -> Acetyl group binds to Coenzyme A

nodes.label: The Krebs Cycle
nodes.description: Located in the mitochondrial matrix, the Krebs Cycle is a cyclical mechanism of oxidation. Throughout this cycle, decarboxylase enzymes remove carbon dioxide, while dehydrogenase enzymes reduce both NAD+ and FAD into NADH and FADH2. A small amount of ATP is also produced via substrate-level phosphorylation.
nodes.pathwaySteps: Acetyl CoA delivers acetyl group -> Binds with 4C Oxaloacetate -> Forms 6C Citrate -> Oxidative Decarboxylation to 5C -> Oxidative Decarboxylation to 4C -> Regeneration of Oxaloacetate

nodes.label: The Electron Transport Chain
nodes.description: A series of trans-membrane protein complexes embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane (cristae). NADH and FADH2 deliver high-energy electrons to these complexes. As electrons are passed down the chain, they lose energy. This energy drives proton pumping, moving hydrogen ions from the matrix into the narrow intermembrane space, building a massive concentration gradient. At the end of the chain, molecular oxygen acts as the final electron acceptor, binding with electrons and free protons to form water, preventing the chain from backing up.
nodes.pathwaySteps: Electrons delivered by NADH/FADH2 -> Electrons passed down chain -> Energy used for proton pumping -> Proton gradient built -> Oxygen accepts electrons + protons -> Water formed

nodes.label: Chemiosmosis
nodes.description: The mechanism that couples the proton gradient to ATP synthesis. Because the inner membrane is impermeable to protons, they must diffuse back into the matrix through a specialized enzyme called ATP Synthase. The process leads to massive ATP production.
nodes.pathwaySteps: Proton Motive Force builds -> Protons flow through ATP Synthase -> Rotor subunit turns -> Conformational change in catalytic knob -> ADP binds with inorganic phosphate -> Massive ATP production
